front: Practice: the daily fast begins when a white thread can be distinguished from a black thread, per a verse in the Cow Surah (Al-Baqarah)
Practice: per hadith, a faster who avoids "forged speech" and "evil actions" may cross a special gate to Paradise called Ar-Rayyan
Practice: per another hadith, this fast serves as a "shield" and multiplies good deeds tenfold
Practice: intentionally missing a day of this fast can be atoned for by feeding sixty impoverished people
Practice: a weak hadith holds that Adam performed this fast on the "White Days" (the 13th-15th of each month) after his skin turned black
Practice: modern fatwas hold that COVID-19 vaccines and Pap smears do not break this fast; violators may make fidya or kaffara donations
Practice: tradition holds that during this month, the gates of Heaven are opened, the gates of Hell are shut, and demons are chained up
Practice: in South India, mung beans/lentils, rice, and coconut milk are made into a porridge called nombu kanji for this meal
Practice: Turkish platters for this meal usually include cheese, dried meat, and a sesame-topped pita
Practice: a baklava-like dessert of thin dough soaked in sweetened milk, gullac, is typically eaten for this meal
Practice: in Hyderabad, a stew of pounded wheat, lentils, and goat meat called haleem is typically eaten for this meal
Practice: an apricot drink called Qamr ad-Din is traditionally consumed with this meal
Practice: town criers called mesaharati traditionally wake people for this meal by walking the streets playing drums; Bilal ibn Rabah is said to have been the first to serve in this role
Practice: in parts of Southeast Asia, a slit drum called the kentongan replaces the mesaharati wake-up call for this meal; Egyptian mesaharati use bazah drums before the fajr prayer
Practice: Sunni Muslims may attempt a full Quran recitation (khatm) during these prayers by reading one juz per night, led by a hafiz
Practice: falls on an odd-numbered night among the last ten days of Ramadan; worship on it is said to be worth more than a thousand months, and it marks when the Quran was first revealed to Muhammad
Practice: devout Muslims may seclude themselves in a mosque during Ramadan in this practice, most common around the Night of Power
Practice: signs said to mark the approach of the Night of Power include a moon resembling half of a plate and a "feeble and red" sunrise with no rays
Practice: Arabic-language TV serials called musalsalat traditionally premiere during Ramadan, replacing the role of older post-iftar oral storytellers; Adel Emam is among their most famous creators
Practice: Egyptian nightly viewership of these serials nearly doubles during Ramadan; 140 new series competed for ad slots in 2017, with Kalabsh a 2018 breakout hit
Practice: the 2015 Ramadan series Harat al-Yehud featured one of the first positive Jewish characters on Egyptian TV, Moussa Haroun
Practice: the Turkish series Muhtesem Yuzyil, depicting Suleiman the Magnificent, was watched by an estimated 150 million people; Erdogan threatened to prosecute its producers
Practice: in Indonesia, Ramadan's end is marked by mudik, a mass homecoming tradition, after which people eat ketupat rice dumplings
Practice: in Indonesia, villagers clean cemeteries beforehand in the nyadran tradition, and ritually bathe in springs in the Padusan tradition
Practice: in Indonesia, employers must pay workers the THR bonus at least a week before Eid al-Fitr
Practice: in Egypt, a cannon is fired and colorful fanoos lanterns are hung to mark Ramadan
Practice: in the Ottoman Empire, silk swings hung between minarets, decorated with lanterns, were a popular Ramadan nighttime pastime, run by Janissaries and paid for with akce coins
Holiday: this month's name derives from a root (ramad) meaning the "scorching heat of the ground"; the word itself appears only once in the Quran (a hapax legomenon)
Holiday: its practices were syncretized with pre-Islamic Sabian customs of worshipping the moon's disappearance and reappearance
Holiday: folklorist Margaret Hasluck noted it is commonly used as a personal name for children born during the month, matching the surname of the Swiss scholar Tariq Ramadan
